course_description:
This is an advanced course in applied macroeconomic research intended for Honours students. The course focuses on the practical skills needed to perform research projects in macroeconomics. The course covers topics such as academic review of literature, data collection, replicability, model selection, application of empirical or quantitative tools, and an effective communication of academic research in macroeconomics.

This course focuses on empirical and quantitative tools for macroeconomic research.

The course is divided into two parts. In Part 1, we study the state of the arts methods used in empirical macroeconomic research. Additionally, we apply these methods to study questions related to students dissertation or interests.

In Part 2, we focus on research communication. This is, writing and presenting macroeconomic research to different audiences.

restrictions: Enrolment restricted to students in the BEcon(Hons), BAdvFinEcon(Hons), BA(Hons - Economics)

Enquiries relating specifically to course content should be directed to the Course Coordinator/Lecturer.

Mid-Semester Break:
29 September - 3 October. Semester 2 classes recommence Tue 7 October.
course_aims: The aim of this course is to provide student with knowledge and practical research experience for macroeconomic research. These include, among others, developing of communications skills, empirical skills, and quantitative tools necessary to perform high quality research in macroeconomics.

learning_outcomes.description: Critically assess gaps in macroeconomic literature.
Design research questions and research projects to fill the gaps.
Apply empirical and/or quantitative tools to advance research projects.
Effective communication of research findings in academic presentation.

assessment_details.title: Empirical Assignment
assessment_details.category: Computer Code, Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Tutorial/ Problem Set
assessment_details.task_description: Students are expected to apply the tools covered in the lectures. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submission via Blackboard by the due date and time. In addition, students should email their codes for the computational part to the lecturer.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Final Research Presentation (Industry Audience)
assessment_details.task_description: Students present their thesis research at the UQ city campus in front of an audience including invited guests from industry, academia, and/or the public sector. Each presentation should be approximately 10 minutes in duration and demonstrate both comprehensive understanding of the student's research and effective application of presentation techniques covered in the presentation skills workshop. Presentations should be accessible to a general academic audience without specialist knowledge of the topic. The presentation should include and demonstrate: Clear articulation of the research question, methodology, and key findings using language accessible to non-specialists Demonstration of critical understanding of the research context and significance Application of presentation techniques taught in the workshop sessions Evaluation Students will be evaluated on factual accuracy, clarity of communication, organisation and structure, time management, effective use of visual aids, ability to effectively communicate their research to the audience, and more generally the demonstration of presentation skills learned throughout the semester. Academic Integrity & AI This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. learning_activities.topic: Empirical Macroeconomic Methods I Basics on Macroeconometric models. Autorregresive models, factor models, and local projection models. Learning outcomes: L01, L03
Empirical Macroeconomic Methods II Identification of macroeconometric models. SVAR and local projections. Learning outcomes: L01, L03
Applications I Extensions of previous models and the methods used by students in their thesis Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
Applications II Extensions of previous models and the methods used by students in their thesis Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
Presentation Skills Training Presentation skills workshop hosted by UQ Academic Skills Advisers Learning outcomes: L01, L04
Writing in Macroeconomics I Critical review of foundational papers and students' writing. Learning outcomes: L01, L04
Writing in Macroeconomics II Critical review of foundational papers and students' writing. Learning outcomes: L01, L04

Students' Presentations I Students present and lecturer provides feedback and review the most common mistakes and most important "rules" of academic communication Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Students' Presentations II Students present and lecturer provides feedback and review the most common mistakes and most important "rules" of academic communication Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Students Presentations Event Students present their thesis research in front of an audience including invited guests from industry, academia, and/or the public sector. Each presentation should be approximately 10 minutes in duration and demonstrate both comprehensive understanding of the student's research and effective application of presentation techniques covered in the presentation skills workshop. Presentations should be accessible to a general academic audience without specialist knowledge of the topic. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Review session Review of course content Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
